title: Christian, Not Pro-Life
author: Brady Butler
orientation: Brady Butler argues that the 'pro-life' label and its incrementalist regulatory approach actually codify a presumed right to abortion, and that Christians should instead pursue the immediate and total abolition of abortion at the state level, citing Oklahoma's failed SB13 as a concrete test case for the strategy.
audiences: Skeptic worried about rape, incest, and life-of-the-mother exceptions
Constitutional conservative interested in 10th Amendment nullification
Pastor or church leader weighing political engagement

questions.question: What did Oklahoma's SB13 actually propose, and why does the author say it required repealing existing pro-life statutes?
questions.answer: SB13 would have recognized a newly conceived child as a human being with unalienable rights and treated killing that child as homicide. To make that prohibition coherent, the bill repealed roughly 20 existing pro-life regulatory statutes, because a law saying 'you cannot murder babies' cannot stand alongside laws that effectively say 'you can murder some babies sometimes.'
questions.quote: SB13 would have repealed close to 20 pro-life statutes from the state's laws. Why was this necessary? You can't have an effective law on the books that says, "you cannot murder babies" and at the same time have a law that says "you can murder some babies sometimes."

questions.question: How does the author respond to the rape and incest exception that even many pro-lifers concede?
questions.audience: Skeptic worried about rape, incest, and life-of-the-mother exceptions
questions.answer: He grants that rape and incest are heinous crimes deserving the fullest punishment of the rapist, but argues the unborn child committed no crime and should not be killed for the sins of the father. The mother is not required to raise the child, but killing the child should never be on the table.
questions.quote: Rape and incest are heinous crimes, but why would we kill the baby for the sins of the father? The child did not commit a crime. If there is anyone to stand up for and protect, it is the child without a loving father. There is never a scenario where the answer is to kill the baby.

questions.question: How does Butler distinguish abortions 'to save the life of the mother' from legitimate medical care like ectopic pregnancy treatment?
questions.audience: Skeptic worried about rape, incest, and life-of-the-mother exceptions
questions.answer: He argues that genuine life-threatening pregnancies (such as ectopic pregnancies) are emergencies handled by trained doctors using medical triage, not scheduled abortions performed by abortionists. Abolition bills like SB13 do not criminalize physicians whose intent is to save life, because abortion as such exists only to end life, never to preserve it.
questions.quote: Abolition bills like SB13 do not criminalize doctors whose intent is to save life. Abortion is never needed to save anyone's life; its only purpose is to cease life.

questions.question: Why does the author say pro-life regulations actually entrench rather than weaken abortion law?
questions.answer: He argues that every regulation implies a right to the underlying act. A 20-week ban implies abortion is permitted before 20 weeks; a forceps ban implies suction abortions are fine. By codifying conditions under which abortion is allowed, pro-life statutes reinforce the assumption that women have a rightful access to abortion in the first place.
questions.quote: The bill that boldly claims that you cannot abort a baby after 20 weeks of gestation implies that you can abort a baby prior to 20 weeks.

questions.question: What is the author's football-field analogy for the difference between pro-life and abolitionist strategy?
questions.answer: He pictures a field with 'ROE' in both endzones and SCOTUS at the 50-yard line. Pro-lifers play yard-by-yard since 1973, gaining a few yards then losing them to lawsuits, with no real reduction in abortions. Abolitionists refuse to play by Roe's rules at all and instead 'cut the lights and turn on the sprinklers,' walking off the field by simply ignoring federal abortion law, as SB13's defiance clause did.
questions.quote: Abolitionists will not play by the rules of *ROE V. Wade*. Abolitionists would simply pull the switch to the stadium lights and turn the sprinklers on as they walk out the gates. SB13 actually had a clause that said all lawsuits, executive orders, and federal statutes regarding legal abortion would be ignored by all Oklahoma agents.

questions.question: Is state defiance of the Supreme Court the same as anarchy?
questions.audience: Constitutional conservative interested in 10th Amendment nullification
questions.answer: No. Butler argues the founders intended states to check and even ignore an out-of-control federal government. He points to Oklahoma already defying Gonzalez v. Raich on medical marijuana, and asks why the state will defy SCOTUS for medical cannabis but not to stop the killing of children.
questions.quote: Did you know that right now Oklahoma is in open defiance to the supreme court decision Gonzalez V. Raich (2005) which states that marijuana is a federally recognized controlled substance and is, therefore, illegal in all 50 states? So, for the love and care of children who need medical marijuana to deal with seizures, we are willing to defy the SCOTUS, but when it comes to preventing the murder of children in the womb we suddenly get weak in the knees and cave like a bunch of pansies!

questions.question: What biblical texts does the author marshal to support abolition over regulation?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader weighing political engagement
questions.answer: He cites Acts 17 (God as author of life), Exodus 20 ('Thou shall not murder'), Psalm 139 (God's omniscience), Jeremiah 7:31 (the murder of children is so irrational it never even entered God's mind), and Proverbs 6 (God hates hands that shed innocent blood). These passages, he says, are the logical and theological foundation of the Abolition Movement.
questions.quote: However, God says that it is so irrational that a mother and father would hire out the murder of their own child that it has never even entered His mind (Jeremiah 7:31). Abortion is so incomprehensible that even the creator Himself cannot rationalize its practice.

questions.question: What concrete change does the author predict if pro-lifers became abolitionists?
questions.answer: He claims a wholesale conversion would end legal abortion in Oklahoma: voters would demand abolition, the legislature would pass it, the governor would sign it, and abortion would shift from a normalized option to a criminal act with real consequences, much like robbing a bank vault.
questions.quote: Put simply, if every abolitionist were to convert back to pro-life tomorrow, absolutely nothing would change in the culture of abortion. However, if every pro-lifer were to become an abolitionist, legalized abortion would end in Oklahoma.

questions.question: Despite the harsh rhetoric, what does the author say is the abolitionist's posture toward those complicit in abortion?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader weighing political engagement
questions.answer: He insists that calling abortion sin is not finger-pointing but a call to repentance, paired with the gospel of Christ crucified. The abolitionist sees himself as a 'wretched worm' apart from grace and offers redemption to anyone, including those who have participated in abortion, who turns to Christ.
questions.quote: Apart from the transforming grace of a loving redeemer the best abolitionist is simply a wretched worm who deserves the full and merciless wrath of God. It is not our intention to judgingly point any fingers to what anyone has done in their past, but rather to call the sin a sin, and point the sinner to the one and only savior, Christ Jesus.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Directly extends Butler's case that incremental regulation legitimizes abortion and that immediate abolition is the only consistent biblical posture.

related.title: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
related.reason: Provides a deeper strategic defense of SB13, the same Oklahoma abolition bill Butler centers his argument on.

related.title: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
related.reason: Addresses the same phenomenon Butler describes: pro-life church leaders opposing the very bill that would abolish abortion.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Names the Oklahoma pro-life legislators Butler accuses of killing SB13 'on a cool day in March.'

related.title: "I am a pro-life Oklahoman who voted to table a bill that would abolish all abortion"
related.reason: Documents a specific Oklahoma senator's vote to table SB13, illustrating the pro-life-majority betrayal Butler highlights.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: The movement's namesake position statement on the same Christian-vs-pro-life distinction Butler makes the title of his essay.

related.title: Randy Brogdon: Righteous Indignation in Response to New York's New Law
related.reason: Echoes Butler's call for state-level defiance and 10th Amendment-grounded resistance to federally protected abortion.
